elements.Text: Use of Smart Card Fare Data to Estimate Public Transport Origin–Destination Matrix
Azalden A. Alsger, Mahmoud Mesbah, Luis Ferreira, and Hamid Safi

Over the past few years, several techniques have been developed for using smart card fare data to estimate origin–destination (O-D) matrices for public transport. In the past, different walking distance and allowable transfer time assumptions had been applied because of a lack of information about the alighting stop for a trip. Such assumptions can significantly affect the accuracy of the estimated O-D matrices. Little evidence demonstrates the accuracy of O-D pairs estimated with smart card fare data. Unique smart card fare data from Brisbane, Queensland, Australia, offered an opportunity to assess previous methods and their assumptions. South East Queensland data were used to study the effects of different assumptions on estimated O-D matrices and to conduct a sensitivity analysis for different parameters. In addition, an algorithm was proposed for generating an O-D matrix from individual user transactions (trip legs). About 85% of the transfer time was non-walking time (wait and short activity time). More than 90% of passengers walked less than 10 min to transfer between alighting and the next boarding stop; this time represented about 10% of the allowable transfer time. A change in the assumed allowable transfer time from 15 to 90 min had a minor effect on the estimated O-D matrices. Most passengers returned to within 800 m of their first origin on the same day.

Smart card fare systems have become a valuable source of information for estimating origin–destination (O-D) matrices in public transport, allowing a better understanding of individual travel patterns, and improving strategic planning for public transport. Use of this information has increased rapidly and may reduce the need for data-collection methods such as household travel surveys and on-board passenger surveys, which have been used for decades to determine the travel patterns of and generate O-D matrices for transit passengers (1–5).

Several recent studies have used different methods to infer the O-D matrices for public transport trips from smart card fare data (6–13). Because most automatic fare collection systems do not require passengers to use their cards when alighting, information about only passenger boarding (not alighting) is recorded. Given the information limitations of some automatic fare collection systems, the accuracy of O-D matrices estimated from their data is uncertain. Also, many assumptions made in such methods are yet to be tested (14).
In the available literature, a passenger’s travel sequence is completed with the trip-chaining method, in which individual user transactions (i.e., trip legs) are connected for each card holder. (The
School of Civil Engineering, University of Queensland, Building 49, Brisbane Saint Lucia, Queensland 4072, Australia. Corresponding author: A.A. Alsger, a.alsger@uq.edu.au.
Transportation Research Record: Journal of the Transportation Research Board, No. 2535, Transportation Research Board, Washington, D.C., 2015, pp. 88–96. DOI: 10.3141/2535-10

assumption is that passengers will not walk far to board at a different public transport stop.)The main objective is to infer the possible alighting stops by creating a buffer zone on the basis of an assumed walking distance around subsequent boarding stops.

The example in Figure 1 illustrates how to implement the trip-chaining method with a buffer zone assumption. A passenger begins Trip 1 at the first boarding location (B1) and ends it at the first alighting location (A1), then walks the second boarding location (B2) to start Trip 2, which ends at the second alighting location (A2). Because alighting information is not available, A1 is estimated by determining whether a previous alighting stop is located in the buffer zone around B2. Most previous studies consider walking time as the main element of the assumption and do not consider nonwalking time part of transfer time. The time between B2 and A1 could be short (enough for a transfer only) or long (perhaps involving an activity).

According to Wang (7) and Zhao et al. (8), an acceptable walking distance is assumed to be 400 m or 5 min of walking time at 4.8 km/h. Any bus stop within walking distance was considered a possible previous alighting stop. Cui assumes that the walking distance between bus stops will not exceed 1,110 m, and any bus stop farther away is not considered (9). Munizaga and Palma propose that the distance a person is willing to walk depends on the type of person, type of city, weather, gradient, and other factors and therefore assume a walking distance of 1,000 m (10). Nassir et al. assume the walking distance is 800 m, and any bus stop located beyond this geographical boundary is not considered (11). This latter walking distance threshold is investigated experimentally in the current paper.

To estimate a passenger O-D trip by connecting trip legs, different values of allowable transfer time (i.e., transfer time thresholds) have been assumed, ranging from 30 min (11, 15) to 60 min (16, 17) and even 90 min (18). Some ranges have been based on transit mode (19). This latter assumption is tested here with observed data.
Rich and unique TransLink data allowed the investigation and evaluation of the effects of three assumptions:
Different transfer time threshold values have specific effects on estimated matrices,
Different buffer zones (walking distances) have specific effects on estimated matrices, and
A passenger’s last destination and first origin locations are the same in a given day.
The generated matrices were compared and evaluated to investigate the effects of different factors on the level of accuracy.

The remaining sections of this paper are organized as follows. First, the O-D estimation method, data, and data preparation are described. Then, a detailed sensitivity analysis is conducted, and the main results are provided. Finally, study conclusions and suggestions for future work are presented.
FIGURE 1 Example of trip chaining; time between B1 and A1 is in-vehicle time.
Method

The proposed approach develops an O-D estimation algorithm on the basis of the trip-chaining method. Assumptions from past studies are evaluated. The main factors in the trip-chaining method are examined in detail with a range of values. Some of the terms used are defined next.
General definitions
Trip leg is the in-vehicle time or distance (part of a trip) between a passenger’s boarding and alighting locations.

An O-D trip describes passenger movement from an origin to a destination by public transport. It may entail one or more trip legs and related transfers. Only the component in the public transport system is considered; access to public transport is not.
Transfer distance is the walking distance to transfer between subsequent trip legs on public transport (i.e., between a passenger’s boarding and alighting locations).
Buffer zone is an assumed area set around a public transport boarding stop that includes any possible public transport alighting stops.
trip-Chaining Method Assumptions

Different studies make different assumptions with the trip-chaining method, but typically, (a) a passenger’s last destination and first origin locations are the same in a given day and (b) the trip origin is the boarding stop and the trip destination is the alighting stop. The major assumption required to connect trip legs and create a passenger trip is a value for the threshold for intertransaction time (ITT); an ITT value shorter than this threshold identifies a transfer. Studies in the literature use many values for this threshold.
Intertransaction time

A combination of passenger walking time and nonwalking time, ITT is the time between Trip 1 alighting (e.g., A1) and Trip 2 boarding (e.g., B2) in Figure 1. Nonwalking time is the combination of waiting time and a possible activity time, also shown in Figure 1.
An ITT must be assumed to obtain a passenger’s travel pattern and will be set as intervals for transfer time. If less than this thresh-
old, the ITT will be considered transfer time; otherwise, the ITT includes an activity and the next trip leg is considered a new trip.
o-d estimation Algorithm

Figure 2 illustrates the algorithm for connecting an individual passenger’s trip legs to estimate the O-D matrix and investigate the assumptions of the trip-chaining method. The main part of this algorithm detects transfers so that the trip legs can be merged (i.e., linked) to obtain an O-D trip. According to this condition, the boarding of first transaction (trip leg) for a unique card ID is an origin. The remaining trip legs for the same card ID will be transfers if the ITT is less than the allowable transfer time. If the ITT exceeds the allowable transfer time, then the last alighting stop is the passenger’s trip destination and the next boarding stop is the origin of a new trip. If it is the last transaction of the current card ID, then choose another card ID and continue the searching algorithm.

Because the transfer time threshold has an important effect on the estimated O-D matrix, different thresholds are analyzed here. The transfer time threshold was increased from 15 min to 90 min in 15-min increments. Trip legs were linked to estimate the O-D matrix, as shown in the top box of Figure 2. The trip-chaining method assumptions [i.e., transfer time thresholds, allowable walking distances (buffer zones), and passenger’s last destination same as first origin in a given day] are investigated.

The generated matrix, based on a 60-min threshold, was set as a reference measurement. In Brisbane, a 60-min threshold is used for fare collection. All other estimated matrices are compared with the reference matrix to quantify the effect of different assumptions about transfer time threshold; as a result, the likely errors in the estimated matrices associated with such different assumptions are quantified.
data description and Preparation

The smart card data analyzed here were obtained from TransLink, the public transport authority of South East Queensland (SEQ), Australia. Data from 1 weekday comprised 628,479 transactions (from 260,803 card holders) over the SEQ bus, train, and ferry network. A transaction record, generated each time a passenger boards or alights, contains the operation date, run, route, direction, ticket number, smart card ID, boarding time, alighting time, boarding stop, and alighting stop.
FIGURE 2 O-D estimation algorithm.

One important aspect of this system is that both boarding and alighting times and locations (i.e., where the passenger gets on or off a public transport vehicle) are included. Transferring activities are not obtained directly. The data were filtered, and some transactions were excluded (e.g., duplicate transactions and transactions for which no boarding or alighting stops were recorded). The initial 1-day data set contained 473,525 transactions after cleaning.
o-d estIMAtIon

The main part of the proposed algorithm is the detection of transfer trip legs. Trip legs cannot be linked until after the ITT threshold is determined. The ITT threshold includes walk time to the next boarding stop, wait time, and possibly—for longer ITTs—activity time. The main challenge is identifying the ITT, in which a trip leg will be considered initial (i.e., trip origin) if the time is greater than the ITT and transfer if the time is less than the ITT. The main results are summarized here, including a sensitivity analysis based on the proposed algorithm for different ITTs. Matrices are
generated from ITT assumptions from 15 to 90 min, at 15-min intervals.

Figure 3 depicts total O-D trips and trips with a transfer. The total number of O-D trips decreases slightly as the ITT increases from 15 to 90 min. In contrast, the number of transfer trips increases as the ITT increases. The percentage of transfer trips increases from 15% to 20% as the ITT increases from 15 to 45 min and increases slightly, from 21% to 23%, as the ITT increases from 60 to 90 min. The nature of demand (i.e., the low portion of transfer trips) indicates that most passengers do not transfer. It also indicates that the assumed transfer time threshold does not have a significant impact on the O-D matrices or the number of transfer trips.

The trip duration distributions of trips with one, two, or three transfers are given in Figure 4. Most O-D trips require one transfer. The number of O-D trips with one transfer increases as trip duration increases and reaches its maximum at a 40-min trip duration. The same can be said about a two-transfer trip, which reaches its maximum at 60 min. Few O-D trips that have three transfers occur after a 30-min trip duration and reach their maximum at 90 min. The number of trips with transfers indicates that most transfer trips have
0 15 30 45 Transfer Threshold (min) 60 75 90 Total O-D trips Transfer trips 15% 18% 20% 21% 22% 23% 50,000 100,000 150,000 200,000Number of O-D Trips 250,000 300,000 350,000
FIGURE 3 Total number of O-D and transfer trips.
only one transfer. The number of trips with two or three transfers increases as trip duration increases, but the number is small compared with one-transfer O-D trips.

The estimated O-D trips were aggregated according to the 1,515 zones in the Brisbane Strategic Transport Model to provide an overview of the results (20). Figure 5 is the geographical analysis of origins and destinations during the morning peak (7 to 9 a.m.) and the evening peak (4 to 6 p.m.) with the 60-min ITT. Morning destinations are concentrated around the central business district (CBD), where most of trips are work trips (Figure 5b), and most evening origins are generated from the CBD (Figure 5c). Morning origins and evening destinations appear to be symmetric, which indicates that passengers’ O-D trips begin and end at the same location. Morning destinations and evening origins also appear to be symmetric, especially around the CBD, which indicates that most
of the O-D trips are work trips, in which passengers travel to work in the morning and travel home in the evening.
testing the First Assumption: transfer time threshold

Figure 6 shows the distributions of average ITT, walking time, and nonwalking time for passengers who made more than one trip. Walking time is calculated as the direct distance between the alighting stop and the next boarding stop at an average walking speed of 5 km/h. Most of the transactions of passengers who made more than one trip leg took place during a 30-min ITT. However, with a transfer, most of the ITT is nonwalking time; walking and nonwalking times increase as the ITT increases (Table 1).

FIGURE 5 Geographical analysis of origins and destinations at zonal level during morning peak (7 to 9 a.m.) and evening peak (4 to 6 p.m.) with 60-min ITT: (a) morning origins, (b) morning destinations, (c) evening origins, and (d) evening destinations.

Average walking and nonwalking times and their percentage of ITT are listed in Table 1 for all studied ITTs. For average ITTs of 0 to 15 min, walk time to the next boarding stop is around 1 min, and nonwalking time is around 5 min. For ITTs of 45 to 60 min, the average walking time is 2.5 min and the average nonwalking time to start the next trip is almost 50 min. The average walking time as a percentage of total ITT decreases from 12.6% to 3.7% as the ITT increases from 0 to 90 min.

The cumulative distribution of the number of transactions is given in Figure 7 as walking time, nonwalking time, and ITT. More than 90% of passengers are willing to walk less than 10 min to transfer between bus stops or train stations. Even with long ITTs, when an activity takes place, walking time is short between the alighting stop and the next boarding stop. Also, the ITT of about 50% of pas-

sengers is 0 to 20 min. TransLink’s 60-min transfer time does not have a significant effect on travel patterns. The investigated transfer time threshold indicates that most of this time is waiting time, because walking time is short.
testing the second Assumption: transfer Walking distance

Average walking distances are given in Table 1 for all studied ITTs. Passengers walk shorter distances when they have less time to transfer to their next boarding stop, which is about 160 m for an ITT of less than 30 min. As the ITT exceeds 45 min, average walking distance changes slightly.

Figure 8 shows the cumulative distribution of walking distances for different ITTs. The walking distance distribution is not significantly affected by ITT, because the distances are short. About 50% of passengers walk less than 100 m to transfer between stops at different ITT thresholds. About 85% of passengers walk less than 650 m to get to their next boarding stop at different ITT thresholds.

Figure 9 compares the results of using different buffer zone assumptions from the literature—400 m (7, 8), 800 m (11), 1,000 m (10), and 1,100 m (9)—and all studied ITTs. Most transfers take place with a short walking distance; for example, the ITT of more than 88% of the transactions is less than 60 min for assumed walking distances of 800, 1,000, and 1,100 m. At 400 m, the ITT is less than 60 min for about 82% of transfers.
The number of transfers does not change when walking distance is assumed to be equal to or greater than 800 m for different ITTs. The number of transfers increases as the ITT increases for different

assumed walking distances. Therefore, the assumed walking distance does not need to be increased beyond 800 m because no significant change results.
testing the third Assumption: Last destination same as First origin

Because no alighting information was available for previous estimations, a passenger’s last destination was assumed to be the same as his or her first origin in a given day. The unique SEQ data, which include boarding and alighting information, allowed testing of this assumption.

The 60-min O-D matrix (i.e., reference matrix) was used, excluding O-D trips with only one trip leg. From the total 109,652 O-D trips, 82% of passengers returned to their first boarding location (first origin) of that day and 88% returned to within a 800-m walking distance of their first boarding location. The distance between the alighting stop and the boarding stop is different from the distance between the alighting or boarding stop and the actual destina-

tion (i.e., place of activity). The typical 800-m access distance in the literature is between the alighting or boarding stop and the destination. As expected, results indicate that most passengers returned to their first origin in a given day (Figure 5, a and d).
ACCurACy LeveL

Widely used for traffic engineering and traffic modeling to compare two sets of traffic volumes, the Geoffrey E. Havers (GEH) statistic is used in this study to evaluate the accuracy of each matrix with different ITT thresholds. Because the formula is applied to every estimated O-D pair in the matrix, the common pitfalls that occur when using simple percentage formulas to compare two O-D matrices are avoided. A GEH statistic of less than 5 indicates a good fit (21). The formula is
xy xy i ii i ( )= − + GEH 2 2

FIGURE 10 Relative errors (versus 60 min) in O-D estimation with all studied ITTs.
where xi is the sampled measurement and yi is the reference measurement.

The generated O-D matrix with a 60-min ITT was considered as a reference (to be compatible with the TransLink 60-min transfer time). The relationship between the errors in each estimated matrix (compared with a 60-min O-D matrix) and its related ITT is shown in Figure 10. Expectedly, the error in the estimated matrix reaches minimum at around 60 min and increases when ITT differs greatly from 60 min (e.g., 15 min). Nevertheless, the highest average GEH for all matrices is less than 0.5, which indicates that differences in the estimated matrices are not significant as ITT changes from 15 to 90 min and that decreasing the ITT (from 60 to 15 min) has a greater effect on the estimated matrices than increasing the ITT (from 60 to 90 min).
ConCLusIons

O-D matrices are a critical foundation for public transportation analysis, design, and management. Such data provide information about the number of travelers between different regional zones that can be used in transportation planning to determine infrastructure and service demand. As a result, the accuracy of estimated O-D matrices is an important issue.

Smart card fare data were used to assess the validity of trip-chaining assumptions on the estimated O-D matrix for public transport. The major assumptions investigated were transfer time thresholds, transfer walking distances, and last destination of a passenger same as first origin in a given day. Most of the ITT is nonwalking time (i.e., waiting time and short activity time). Changing the transfer time set by TransLink does not have a visible impact on passenger travel patterns. More than 90% of passengers are willing to walk less than 10 min to transfer between transit services.

An algorithm for estimating O-D matrices was applied with different assumptions for ITT. The estimated matrices changed slightly as the ITT increased from 15 to 90 min. The increased ITT allowed more trip legs to be joined, which increased the number of transfer trips from 15% to 23% as the ITT increased from 15 to 90 min. Of all transfer trips, 91% have only one transfer (for ITT = 60 min), which indicates that the assumed allowable transfer time does not have a significant impact on the O-D matrices or the number of transfer trips.
Most passengers return to their first origin in a given day. This assumption includes some errors, because some passengers might

use other travel modes (private cars) or end the day at different destinations. The accuracy of the estimated matrices remains a concern because the actual O-D matrix is not known. The GEH statistic was used to evaluate the accuracy of each matrix with different ITTs. The generated 60-min O-D matrix was set as a reference to calculate the relevant errors. The calculated relative error shows no significant effect on the estimated matrix as ITT changes from 15 to 90 min. These results suggest that the allowable transfer time could be increased because it does not significantly affect the number of O-D trips or, therefore, revenue. However, an increased allowable transfer time could encourage more people to use public transport services.

Each of the previous assumptions (walking distance, allowable transfer time, and last destination same as first origin) includes some errors, which are quantified here. This work can be extended to compare other estimation methods that use data from smart card systems in other cities. Previous estimation methods and their related assumptions can be applied to the SEQ public transport system, and results can be evaluated and compared with results from this study.
ACknoWLedGMents

The authors acknowledge grant support from the Australian Research Council. The authors also thank TransLink (the public transport authority of South East Queensland, Australia) for providing the data for this research.
